title: Fevers and chills after a trip abroad

chart.cc: Fevers, chills, and headache since returning from travel
chart.oneLiner: 34M presents with 5 days of cyclical fevers, rigors, headache, and myalgias that began about a week after returning from West Africa.

opening: I keep getting these terrible fevers and shaking chills since I got back from my trip, and my whole body aches.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 34-year-old man, flushed and diaphoretic, looks unwell and uncomfortable but is alert. Good historian.

HPI: 5 days of fevers reaching 39-40°C with drenching sweats and bone-shaking rigors, often coming and going every day or two. Severe frontal headache, diffuse myalgias, fatigue, poor appetite, and a few episodes of nausea and loose stools. Returned ~9 days ago from a 3-week trip to rural Nigeria (West Africa) visiting family (only if asked about travel). Took a few doses of malaria prophylaxis early in the trip but stopped because of nausea and did NOT complete it (only if asked specifically about prophylaxis/pills to prevent malaria). Slept in a village without bed nets or air conditioning and had many mosquito bites (only if asked about mosquito exposure/precautions). NO cough, NO dysuria, NO neck stiffness, NO rash, NO bleeding, NO confusion currently. No sick contacts with similar illness.
PMH: healthy, no chronic illness. MEDS: stopped antimalarial prophylaxis (if asked); no other regular meds. ALLERGIES: none. SOCIAL: works as an engineer; minimal alcohol; no injection drug use; monogamous. IMMUNIZATIONS: had yellow fever and typhoid vaccines before travel, hepatitis A series complete (if asked about vaccines).

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: ill-appearing, flushed, diaphoretic, febrile; alert and fully oriented (NO confusion/obtundation).
- Vitals: BP 104/66, HR 118, RR 22, 39.4°C, SpO2 96% RA.
- Abdomen: mild splenomegaly and mild tenderness in the left upper quadrant if examined; no peritoneal signs.
- HEENT/Skin: mild scleral icterus if examined; NO rash, NO petechiae, NO eschar, NO jaundice of the skin.
- Neuro: alert and oriented, no meningismus, no focal deficit.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the travel history, incomplete prophylaxis, or mosquito exposure unless asked about travel, pills/precautions to prevent malaria, or insect exposure.
- Splenomegaly and scleral icterus are revealed only if the abdomen/eyes are examined.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.

labs.BloodSmear: Thick and thin Giemsa-stained blood smears: intraerythrocytic ring-form trophozoites of Plasmodium falciparum; parasitemia ~3% on the thin film (quantified). NO Schuffner dots / no banana-shaped gametocytes mandatory but falciparum favored.
labs.RDT: Rapid diagnostic test POSITIVE for P. falciparum HRP-2 antigen (confirms species while smear is being read).
labs.CBC: Hgb 10.8, Plt 78 (thrombocytopenia — common and supportive), WBC normal (no leukocytosis). Mild anemia.
labs.Chemistry: Cr 1.0 (normal renal function), glucose 92 (no hypoglycemia), lactate 1.8 (normal) — these screen for SEVERE malaria; bilirubin mildly elevated (indirect, from hemolysis).
labs.BloodCultures: Bacterial blood cultures drawn and PENDING — NO growth (helps exclude typhoid/enteric fever and bacterial sepsis as the cause).

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Sinus tachycardia with an otherwise normal tracing; note the baseline QTc because some antimalarials (e.g., quinine, chloroquine, lumefantrine) prolong the QT interval.
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: Sinus tachycardia with an otherwise normal tracing; useful as a baseline QTc before QT-prolonging antimalarials. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) — © Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.
ekg.imageDescription: 12-lead showing sinus tachycardia with normal intervals (baseline QTc before antimalarials)

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Clear lung fields with no consolidation or effusion; new bilateral infiltrates would raise concern for malaria-associated ARDS/pulmonary edema, a feature of SEVERE malaria.
cxr.verify: Source a CC chest film that is clear/normal, OR one showing bilateral infiltrates (ARDS); label which it is. Self-host with appropriate credit.
cxr.imageDescription: chest radiograph with clear lung fields (screening for malaria-associated ARDS)

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential for this febrile returning traveler (note your leading diagnosis).
steps.rubric: Malaria (leading & CANT-MISS,9). Typhoid/enteric fever (CANT-MISS,3). Viral hemorrhagic fever / arboviral illness (dengue, etc.) (core,3). Bacterial sepsis/occult bacteremia (core,2). Acute viral hepatitis or other cosmopolitan infection (core,2). Connects fever pattern + endemic travel + incomplete prophylaxis + splenomegaly/thrombocytopenia to malaria and states malaria must be excluded urgently in any febrile traveler (core,6).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Malaria. Must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: Typhoid/enteric fever. Also reasonable: Viral hemorrhagic fever / arboviral illness (dengue); Bacterial sepsis/occult bacteremia; Acute viral hepatitis or other cosmopolitan infection.

coreActions.item: Acute viral hepatitis or other cosmopolitan infection
Connects fever pattern + endemic travel + incomplete prophylaxis + splenomegaly/thrombocytopenia to malaria and states malaria must be excluded urgently in any febrile traveler

acceptableAlternatives: Acute viral hepatitis or other cosmopolitan infection

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: Thick and thin blood smears for malaria parasites with species ID and parasitemia (CANT-MISS,5). Rapid diagnostic test (RDT) for malaria antigen (CANT-MISS,3). CBC with platelets (2). Chemistry/glucose/lactate/bilirubin to assess for SEVERE malaria (2). Blood cultures to exclude typhoid/bacterial sepsis (2). Repeat smears q12-24h x3 if first is negative (1). PENALTY: ruling out malaria on a single negative smear without repeating (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: Thick and thin blood smears for malaria parasites with species ID and parasitemia; Rapid diagnostic test (RDT) for malaria antigen; Chemistry/glucose/lactate/bilirubin to assess for SEVERE malaria.

criticalActions.item: Thick and thin blood smears with species ID and parasitemia
Rapid diagnostic test (RDT) for malaria antigen

penalties.item: ruling out malaria on a single negative smear without repeating q12-24h x3

requiredTests: Thick and thin blood smears with species ID and parasitemia
Rapid diagnostic test (RDT) for malaria antigen
contraindicatedTests: ruling out malaria on a single negative smear without repeating q12-24h x3
orderSensitiveActions: ruling out malaria on a single negative smear without repeating q12-24h x3

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings (consider what you need before treatment).
steps.rubric: Findings = sinus tachycardia, otherwise normal; document a baseline QTc. CREDIT: rate & rhythm (5); recognizes a normal/near-normal tracing with sinus tachycardia (5); notes the need for baseline QTc before QT-prolonging antimalarials (quinine, chloroquine, lumefantrine) (3); systematic read (2). PENALTY: missing that the QT matters before antimalarial therapy (-2).
keyFindings: notes the need to document baseline QTc before QT-prolonging antimalarials
criticalFindings: Sinus tachycardia with an otherwise normal tracing; document the baseline QTc because some antimalarials prolong the QT interval.
commonMistakes: ignoring the QT interval before starting an antimalarial that prolongs it
steps.idealAnswer: Sinus tachycardia with an otherwise normal tracing; document the baseline QTc because antimalarials (quinine, chloroquine, lumefantrine) can prolong the QT.

criticalActions.item: Recognizes: Sinus tachycardia with an otherwise normal tracing; document the baseline QTc before QT-prolonging antimalarials.

coreActions.item: notes the need to document baseline QTc before QT-prolonging antimalarials

penalties.item: ignoring the QT interval before starting an antimalarial that prolongs it

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = clear lungs; new bilateral infiltrates would suggest malaria-associated ARDS (a severe feature). CREDIT: identifies a clear/normal film (6); recognizes that bilateral infiltrates would indicate malaria-associated ARDS / severe disease (4). PENALTY: inventing pneumonia/consolidation that is not present (-2).
keyFindings: recognizes that bilateral infiltrates would indicate malaria-associated ARDS / severe disease
criticalFindings: Clear lung fields with no consolidation or effusion; new bilateral infiltrates would raise concern for malaria-associated ARDS/pulmonary edema (severe malaria).
commonMistakes: inventing pneumonia/consolidation that is not present
steps.idealAnswer: Clear lung fields; new bilateral infiltrates would raise concern for malaria-associated ARDS/pulmonary edema, a feature of severe malaria.

criticalActions.item: Recognizes: Clear lung fields with no consolidation or effusion; new bilateral infiltrates would indicate malaria-associated ARDS (severe malaria).

coreActions.item: recognizes that bilateral infiltrates would indicate malaria-associated ARDS / severe disease

penalties.item: inventing pneumonia/consolidation that is not present

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Blood smear shows intraerythrocytic ring forms of P. falciparum at ~3% parasitemia, RDT positive, thrombocytopenia, mild anemia, with normal creatinine, glucose, and lactate. What is your diagnosis and what did you rule out?
steps.rubric: Malaria — Plasmodium falciparum (6). Cites positive smear/RDT with species and parasitemia; explicitly assesses severity (here UNCOMPLICATED: parasitemia <5%, no end-organ dysfunction, no cerebral/renal/pulmonary involvement, normal lactate/glucose) and notes blood cultures help exclude typhoid/bacterial sepsis (4).
steps.idealAnswer: Malaria due to Plasmodium falciparum (currently uncomplicated).

steps.label: Management
steps.rubric: Per MGH approach to malaria: confirm species and parasitemia and assess severity FIRST (CANT-MISS,5); for uncomplicated falciparum start an oral artemisinin-based combination therapy (ACT, e.g., artemether-lumefantrine or atovaquone-proguanil) (CANT-MISS,6); for SEVERE malaria give IV artesunate (4); admit and monitor parasitemia and for complications (hypoglycemia, ARDS, AKI, cerebral malaria) (4); Infectious Disease consult and report to public health (3); supportive care and antipyretics, follow QTc on therapy (2). PENALTY: treating presumed falciparum with chloroquine alone in a region of widespread resistance (-3); discharging a patient with falciparum malaria without confirming species/severity (-1).
steps.idealAnswer: Confirm species and quantify parasitemia and assess severity first. Uncomplicated P. falciparum: oral artemisinin-based combination therapy (ACT — artemether-lumefantrine, or atovaquone-proguanil). Severe malaria (parasitemia ≥5%, impaired consciousness, shock, ARDS, AKI, acidosis, hypoglycemia, or significant bleeding): IV artesunate, admit to a monitored/ICU setting. Admit and follow serial parasitemia and complications; treat hypoglycemia; supportive care and antipyretics; monitor QTc on QT-prolonging agents. Infectious Disease consult and notify public health. Do NOT use chloroquine monotherapy for falciparum from a resistant region.

criticalActions.item: Confirm species/parasitemia and assess severity first
Artemisinin-based combination therapy for uncomplicated falciparum

coreActions.item: Admit and monitor parasitemia and for complications
Infectious Disease consult and public health notification
Supportive care/antipyretics and follow QTc on therapy

bonusActions.item: Counsel on prophylaxis adherence and bed nets for future travel

penalties.item: Chloroquine monotherapy for falciparum from a resistant region

requiredActions: Confirm species/parasitemia and assess severity first
Artemisinin-based combination therapy for uncomplicated falciparum
optionalActions: Admit and monitor parasitemia and for complications
Infectious Disease consult and public health notification
Supportive care/antipyretics and follow QTc on therapy
Counsel on prophylaxis adherence and bed nets for future travel
unsafeActions: Chloroquine monotherapy for falciparum from a resistant region

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 34M presents with 5 days of cyclical fevers, rigors, headache, and myalgias that began about a week after returning from West Africa. The diagnosis is Malaria in a returning traveler, reached by eliciting the focused travel/exposure history, performing the targeted exam, and interpreting the directed workup (blood smear, RDT, severity labs) while excluding the must-not-miss alternatives.
keyHistoryClues: travel to a malaria-endemic region (West Africa)
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Abdomen: mild splenomegaly and mild tenderness in the left upper quadrant if examined; no peritoneal signs.
HEENT/Skin: mild scleral icterus if examined; no rash, no petechiae, no eschar.
General: ill-appearing, flushed, diaphoretic, febrile; alert and fully oriented.
Neuro: alert and oriented, no meningismus, no focal deficit (NO confusion/obtundation to suggest cerebral malaria).
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including sensitive items (travel, prophylaxis, exposures) only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal key findings (splenomegaly, mental status, scleral icterus).
Build a differential led by Malaria while explicitly excluding must-not-miss causes (typhoid/enteric fever).
Order a focused workup (thick/thin smears, RDT, severity labs) and interpret EKG/CXR and labs.
Commit to Malaria, assess severity, and manage with species-appropriate therapy, monitoring, consults, and disposition.
commonPitfalls: Chloroquine monotherapy for falciparum from a resistant region
Ruling out malaria on a single negative smear without repeating
managementPearls: Confirm species/parasitemia and assess severity first
Artemisinin-based combination therapy for uncomplicated falciparum
criticalWorkupItems: Thick and thin blood smears with species ID and parasitemia
Rapid diagnostic test (RDT) for malaria antigen
criticalInterpretationItems: Sinus tachycardia with an otherwise normal tracing; document the baseline QTc before QT-prolonging antimalarials.
Clear lung fields with no consolidation or effusion; new bilateral infiltrates would indicate malaria-associated ARDS (severe malaria).
criticalManagementItems: Confirm species/parasitemia and assess severity first
Artemisinin-based combination therapy for uncomplicated falciparum
keyImageInterpretationFindings: Sinus tachycardia with an otherwise normal tracing; document the baseline QTc before QT-prolonging antimalarials.
Clear lung fields with no consolidation or effusion; new bilateral infiltrates would indicate malaria-associated ARDS (severe malaria).
criticalImageInterpretationFindings: Sinus tachycardia with an otherwise normal tracing; document the baseline QTc before QT-prolonging antimalarials.
Clear lung fields with no consolidation or effusion; new bilateral infiltrates would indicate malaria-associated ARDS (severe malaria).
imageInterpretationPearls: An incidental abnormality is an interpretation task in its own right — name it, then judge its relevance.
Read every study systematically before committing.
State pertinent negatives, not just positives.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
